title: 2025 05 15 Petitions
displayTitle: 2025-05-15 - Petitions Committee - Briefing on the petition of Kylie Whellan: Urgently establish a children’s select committee
topic: Briefing on the petition of Kylie Whellan: Urgently establish a children’s select committee
description: Thursday, 15 May 2025 Petitions Committee Briefing on the petition of Kylie Whellan: Urgently establish a children’s select committee Kylie Whellan Petition of Alan Roy Peacock: Limit local authority rate increases to a maximum of the annual inflation rate Alan Roy Peacock Local Government of New Zealand Campbell Barry, Mayor - Lower Hutt Liam Doyle, Senior Policy & Advocacy Advisor
descriptionSummary: Briefing on the petition of Kylie Whellan: Urgently establish a children’s select committee. Kylie Whellan. Petition of Alan Roy Peacock: Limit local authority rate increases to a maximum of the annual inflation rate

chunks.text: Make an effort, um, as well to come and, uh, for here today. Um, and now you, I think you understand the, uh, we have 10 minutes. Um, take your submission as read. Mm-hmm. Ish Campbell from Ireland. Um, I now live in Christ too. Alright, now the floor is yours. It's kind of mine, but also gonna be a bit of yours because as you said, I've submitted my, um, submission. Um, and, and I am gonna go on the lines that you've read that.

Um, so I guess I'm gonna start off with, um, a question of why not, um, if we take the resources excuse, I'll call it excuse off the table, why not have a children's select committee? That's my question to you guys. Who would like to answer it? Or does everyone want to?

No, we are here to hear from you. Um, so you, your job is to convince us why we should, and then what we do is we will look at the other evidence we may even hear from someone else. Then we go away and decide that from what we've heard, that uh, we are happy with that. So you tell us why we should, that's the way it works here. Alright, Great. Then I'll go straight into the implementation for the convention on the rights of a child.

As part of that agreement which New Zealand has signed, there is a, um, coordinated coordination around the implementation that says, um, that the lack of available resources cannot be used as a reason for not establishing programs or safety nets.
So I think that's a really key thing to consider when you are deliberating. Um, if we go to each of the articles, the 42 articles, let me just find them amongst my company.

That's alright. I bought a whole lot of stuff, so it, alright, I've got notes here. A little daunting here. So, article three, article three. Under that, um, convention says that when adults make decisions affecting children, they must think about what is in the best interest of the child. Politicians, authorities in the courts must consider how their decisions affect children, whether it's one child or many.

Now, that to me means that most decisions that are being made and going through existing select committees should be considering that I would venture that that is not happening at the extent that it should. Um, one of my questions was gonna be, would you estimate the amount of time that children are considered when you're making decisions on funding for mental health?

Considering New Zealand that has the worst statistics. We are last on the table in the OECD for child youth suicides, but that's disgusting. It really is. We should be appalled, but that's where we stand. Like, I'm even, sorry, I'm going, I'm getting tingles now from it because it just appalls me that a country that over-indexes on such good stuff like sport, um, you know, some of our AC academic, um, people, our artists, we do really, really well in some areas.

Yet we are last on the table for that. And that's, that's really poignant. That's children killing themselves. We are the worst country in the OECD. Now my petition for establishing that committee in this instance would apply more focus on children for funding when it comes to mental health.

Our mental health system is broken at the moment. I know overall it's broken. We've gotta start somewhere. It's pretty, it's gonna be, it's a big thing to think about kids, but why can't we start with them? Because the future outlook for that is if we are helping them out now, that's less we have to spend later. And that goes across the board for anything.

If we are looking at harsher penalties, why don't what harsher penalties for children that shouldn't be happening. We should be looking at how can we teach these kids? How can we support adults and parents to be a community, raise these kids and, and come together.

You know, the old school way of the communities coming together and looking out for each other is gone. That is gone. New Zealand should have that back because as part of that, and I'm sure Greg, you would understand that being in your previous career of being a police officer where you know all of the rat bags, um, around town and get involved with them and, and proactively get involved.

Um, there's just not enough of that happening. I can see the Children's Select committee looking at policing and going, where, where are we missing the children that are falling through the cracks in our police system? Justice, where, what's happening with our children?

We have an amazing organization in Masterton called the WA Upper Boxing Academy, and I do implore you to look into them. They engage with kids at all age levels, um, in all backgrounds. They came down and supported the local children's day wearing their pink fluoro vests.

All of them equal. They set up all the gazebos. So these kids come out and do work in the community with no expectation of a return as part of their academy. So the boxing academy isn't just about boxing, it's about engaging in a sport. It's about exercise, it's about discipline, but they also encourage them to get out in the community and give back.

Now, organizations like that intervening at an earlier age is what we should be looking at and investing in. I think investment wise, whatever time is spent in the finance committee, investment wise, I've seen funding $6 million worth of funding go to an organization and master in that won't be named that operated for six years, didn't do their job and then didn't get a continuing contract.

Now we talk about resources. There's resources being wasted. A children's select committee can look specifically at funding that's going to these types of organizations where there is no outcomes. To me, in my previous business background, we called it shrinkage.

Now if we looked at the percentage shrinkage of government and took that and put it where there's better outcomes, what would the future look like? Less spend on justice, less spend in the prisons, less spend on health, less spend in mental health area.

It's across the board. It is long term. It's big picture thinking. It's not this is going to get me in voted in the next election. It is big picture. We need to do this to improve our social fabric. I dunno what time we are actually now. Well, We've got about, uh, two or three minutes left.

Okay. If you, if you, if you wanted to leave time for questions, it would probably be good time to start summing up now, but it's up to you. It's Your time. Can I, uh, can I just go then straight to article 42 of the Convention of Rights to Children of, um, rights of a Child.

You have the right to information and knowledge about your rights. Parents and other adults should know about the convention on the rights of the child. Right.

Alright. Thank you. Thank you Kylie, for caring so much about our tam. Something that we all on this committee, uh, really care about. We want to country where our kids are safe. They've got stability and that aha they deserve. Um, so I am really guided by that UN rights the child.

So you would've seen a submission, so I won't, you know, sort of go through that, but I thought I would just touch on some key points. Um, first off, I'll just say that local government New Zealand, uh, generally opposes a, a rates cap. Now we certainly don't oppose working together and how we can bring costs down families and some of the issues that Alan raised around the bite that is happening out there are absolutely accurate.

Um, but we don't believe a rates cap is, is the way to be able to do that. Um, there's a few reasons for that. Uh, s and p uh, have just recently said that, um, rates capping, uh, potential of a rates cap, uh, could impact council's borrowing abilities. So basically making it more expensive, the borrower having interest rates a bit higher.

Um, we also know, uh, that rates, uh, rises, uh, really have come from, um, in more recent times pretty, uh, structural problems like underinvestment and water infrastructure and other mainly infrastructure issues, uh, around the country as well. Um, overseas we've also seen that rates capping, uh, can often have the unintended consequence, uh, of having an underinvestment in that core basic infrastructure as well.

And in places like New South Wales is what you've seen, uh, is that they might have a rates cap, they might keep that rates down for a little bit and then you suddenly have a correction, um, or a spike, uh, in the level of rates because they've realized, gosh, this hasn't quite worked. Um, and you end up actually having more expensive rates generally 'cause you're trying to then play catch up on all the underinvestment that's occurred.

Um, you also have the risk of service cuts, um, which has happened elsewhere. Um, and, and I think, uh, you know, ultimately, um, I understand that, you know, I understand politics a bit. Um, it, it is good politics, um, I think to be able to send that message of keeping rates down the likes, but it is not good policy and it will exacerbate some of the issues, structural issues, like I say, um, that are facing local government.

Um, the other thing I would mention is that I, I do differ from Alan's point of view around, um, local democracy. I think a rates can, it could actually kill local democracy because you're limiting, um, ultimately council's abilities to, to, to make decisions on behalf of their communities. Uh, and the impact that that can have, it certainly would be a further, you know, wouldn't be a movement towards localism or would be a movement away.

Um, and we do think there are better options. So I think we would agree that government has been talking about benchmarking, for example. I think if you get the benchmarking settings right, actually that's a great opportunity, um, to be able to encourage better efficiencies, being able to compare your performance versus other councils, uh, and to see how you can do things better.

So we are certainly not opposed to change and how we can actually, um, help drive some of those costs down. Uh, we just don't want to go down a path of, um, something we will actually make things worse in the long run. Um, yeah, and also the mention of, uh, I suppose capping it to, um, CPI inflation, um, you know, build building, um, a bridge is quite different to baking a loaf of bread.
